bullets.text: Progressive posted its 24th underwriting profit in 30 years with an 8.5% margin in 1996, well above the 4% target; operating income rose 40% to $309.1 million ($4.08/share); ROE was 20.5%.

Net premiums written increased 18% to $3,441.7 million from $2,912.8 million in 1995, with Core business NPW up 19% to $3,367.2 million; Progressive believed it became the 6th largest U.S. auto insurer.
Underwriting expense ratio improved to 21.6% of premiums in 1996 from 23.0% in 1995 and 35.0% in 1990; Gainsharing represented 10.8% of total compensation.
Standard and preferred risks reached 10-15% of 1996 premium volume, up from 5-10% in prior years; Progressive expanded to 43 states with the complete product program offering universal acceptance.

Portfolio grew to $4,450.6 million; issued $100 million of 7.30% 10-year Notes; redeemed preferred shares for $82.1 million; repurchased 1.0 million Common Shares at average $41.73; debt-to-capital ratio was 32%.
Progressive reorganized into 38 local business units by early 1997 (versus 16 at end of 1995), moving profit and growth responsibility closer to communities and customers.

Progressive shifted the underwriting profit target from a per-program annual basis to a 4% margin over the entire retention period of a policyholder, driving focus on customer retention alongside rate adequacy.
Progressive accelerated Gainsharing incentives to reward customer additions and retention, and increased spending to promote the brand and add claim adjusters and customer service representatives.

CEO Peter Lewis reflected on Progressive's evolution in its 60th anniversary year, noting plans for 1997 focused on claim handling quality, brand building, and responding to community-specific opportunities.
